summary: Covers the one difference between -er and -ir verbs (we form: -emos vs -imos), introduces lo + adjective for 'the [adjective] thing', lo de for 'the thing about', and así for 'like this/like that'.

teachings.summary: The only difference in the entire Spanish language between -er and -ir verbs is the 'we' form in the present tense: -emos for -er verbs, -imos for -ir verbs. In all other forms and tenses, they behave identically.
teachings.rule: -er verbs: we = -emos (comemos). -ir verbs: we = -imos (vivimos). This is the ONLY difference between -er and -ir in all of Spanish.
teachings.notes: All other forms use the same endings: come/vive (he), comen/viven (they), etc.

teachings.summary: Lo + adjective means 'the [adjective] thing'. Lo interesante = the interesting thing. Lo fácil = the easy thing. Lo bueno = the good thing. Lo malo = the bad thing.
teachings.rule: Lo + adjective = the [adjective] thing. The adjective stays in masculine singular form.

examples.spanish: Lo interesante es que viven aquí
examples.english: The interesting thing is that they live here

examples.spanish: Lo interesante es que viven juntos
examples.english: The interesting thing is that they live together

examples.spanish: Lo fácil es que vienen juntos
examples.english: The easy thing is that they come together

examples.spanish: Lo complicado es que vienen juntos
examples.english: The complicated thing is that they come together

teachings.notes: Lo bueno = the good thing, lo malo = the bad thing. Very common and useful structure.

teachings.summary: Lo de means 'the thing about' or 'that business about'. Lo de la fiesta = that thing about the party. Lo de Juan = that business about Juan / Juan's business. Lo de mañana = that thing about tomorrow.
teachings.rule: Lo de + noun/name = the thing about [X], that matter of [X]

examples.spanish: No quiero hablar de lo de Juan así
examples.english: I don't want to talk about Juan's business like this
I want to organize that thing about tomorrow
But I don't want to organize it like this

teachings.notes: Lo de is used very often in Spanish. See it as a unit meaning 'that thing about'.

teachings.summary: Así means 'like this' or 'like that' and replaces the longer de esta forma / de esta manera. Very common in everyday Spanish.

examples.english: I don't want to organize it like this

teachings.notes: More common than de esta forma or de esta manera. You'll hear it all the time.

vocabulary.derivation: la mañana = the morning; mañana (no article) = tomorrow
